Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Elliot covers the business side of food and wine. "Standard pitches at least at Bloomberg are doomed to failure almost automatically. We are not allowed to accept pitches. It's not ethical. We generate our own story ideas and use PR firms to confirm or deny information," he says. "But if PR people would just learn to say quickly `here's something you might want to know about' with emphasis on might, and not ordering or instructing a reporter what to do versus gently placing it in front of them so the reporter or editor thinks its his or her idea, that's what a smart PR person does," he adds. "A press release should just be the most basic info in the shortest format. Anything that smacks of pitch is usually deleted at Bloomberg. For basic info, give us as much time as possible. If it's an event our editors might consider newsworthy, the longer the lead time the better," he believes. For interviews, an even longer lead time is better because studio time gets booked up fast," he advises. "Only put the actual story, i.e. Jamie Oliver in NYC Nov. 1-3 in the subject line so we know it's not spam."

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> "The appetite of radio is much greater - we have all news and news-talk stations that are hungry for much more and very different information, and in more depth," he asserts. In essence, "You should pitch to radio more things than you'd pitch to TV because we'll take more. We're in a lot of places that TV isn't. More people get their news from radio than anywhere else, so to not prioritize radio is just silly. But your news has to be on-point," Davis says, explaining that radio audiences have shorter attention spans than other media consumers. In addition, listeners tune in for focused topical relevance, not evergreen fluff. "So use your judgment and pay attention to what's going on in the world," he advises. "Generally, we care a lot about politics and government - how things work, even when there's not an election," Davis says. "There are Fox-type stories that we think of as common-sense stories: the 'how could the government be doing this to you' kind of stories, along with other counter-intuitive pieces that make you wonder about [the world] when you hear them." Keys to coverage: 1) Offer a specific story angle. 2) Think aurally -supply the sound of the news. 3) Have your source standing by. "We generally prefer E-mail for day-in and day-out material to the main address. But if you think you have something that demands our immediate attention, call our assignment desk; but only call with breaking news. You'll get about ten seconds of our attention and you must be prepared. I'm looking for breaking news with national scope, and I'm never interested in phone calls for feature-story pitches."

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Timing is everything in the fast-paced world of radio, and if you can stay a step ahead of the news cycle, you're likely to catch the attention of Kaye.  "It's often not your hard story that gets you on - it's a reaction to something that's happening," he says. "For example, if you work for a large corporation and know the latest unemployment figures are coming out Friday morning at 8:30 a.m., and your company is about to add 500 positions to the payroll, that's the ideal time to pitch. Be inventive, always think ahead and be prepared to get your folks on the air when news breaks." He describes the pitch process as "pitches generally come to a central location, which is usually my E-mail account. I might then suggest that a regional correspondent in Orlando, LA or Greensboro [or wherever your news originates] work on the story. We also have staff reporters in New York and DC who develop spot news and feature material for use in our broadcasts." Kaye suggest pitching stories that will be happening, or spokespeople to comment on upcoming stories, by advance via E-mail. Knowing when a story may run is also key: "We're more likely to do a back-of-the-book story on a Sunday morning or holiday than we would on a weekday," he informs. "You should be aware of slow news days. Also, as a general rule, summer months are slower than winter months and we have more folks available to talk about back-of-the-book stories at 8 p.m. than we do at 8 a.m."  Although he is the News Executive Producer for radio, he is based at CBS News headquarters. Kaye is the primary contact for press releases and story ideas. Topics of interest include spot news, politics, crime, health, entertainment, books, videos and computer news. ""Pitches should be targeted and concise and the client clearly identified,"" Kaye says. Contact by email or fax preferred. Calls are best Tue.-Thu., 1-3 p.m. ""Targeted pitches that clearly identify the client are suggested."" Send a press release, press kit, product, demo/sample, backgrounder or story pitch.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> "Our main focus is on consumer-oriented business news--not in-depth, hard-investor-numbers business news," says Sikora. "We try to take a report and make it understandable for the guy in the street - which is why we like to see a consumer angle." Pitch all stories to the assignment desk. "The assignment desk is the gatekeeper of info, and that's the place to go - right to the editor on duty, who has an overview of what stories are being covered, what's coming, what's breaking, what tapes we have in and what we don't. That person decides whether to go for it immediately, whether to go for it all, or maybe to put it in a file to get to later on." More tips for coverage: 1) Highlight the consumer peg. 2) Offer articulate sources. 3) Stay on point--or be prepared to re-interview. Pitch: "Send all info to radio.editor@wsj.com," says Sikora. "You can follow-up or offer breaking news by phone."

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Bellinger is responsible for breaking news coverage on radio stations around the country that air on the network's business reports. "I look for anything with a business or financial focus that will have an impact on the market's performance," he says. "We interview all types of experts when their field is relevant to what we're reporting on. The best PR pros look at what's ahead in the news and let us know if someone they represent is available to comment. The window of opportunity is fleeting in radio. PR people need to coordinate with their clients to make sure they can be reached for interviews on news release dates. So many firms fail to have someone available who is able or authorized to comment on the day they release a good story. The best way to pitch me is to send an email. If you call, we'll just ask you to send the email so we can discuss it." He recently used a pitch from a PR person who made advance arrangements for her economist client to be available for comment right after a Federal Reserve interest rate announcement.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Topics should appeal to an older, ""high-income and highly educated"" audience, says Chin. They will consider ""everything we can get our hands on,"" if it is presented in an interesting way. Likes obscure, off-beat items. ""If it's a small business story that has a general interest, we'll do it,"" but avoid pitches that are ""too insider."" Seeks popular science topics, cutting-edge research, government and general-interest economic stories. ""We do a lot of health and science,"" he notes. Pitch upbeat stories: ""We like to do positive stories that will bring a smile to someone -- stories of inspiration."" However, they also cover ""obituaries, plane crashes, war stories"" and other such topics. Don't bother pitching experts. Pitch by mail; faxes tend to be thrown out. Sum up your idea in the first few sentences and don't send too much information. Lead is 1-2 days.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Bellinger is responsible for breaking news coverage on radio stations around the country that air on the network's business reports. "I look for anything with a business or financial focus that will have an impact on the market's performance," he says. "We interview all types of experts when their field is relevant to what we're reporting on. The best PR pros look at what's ahead in the news and let us know if someone they represent is available to comment. The window of opportunity is fleeting in radio. PR people need to coordinate with their clients to make sure they can be reached for interviews on news release dates. So many firms fail to have someone available who is able or authorized to comment on the day they release a good story. The best way to pitch me is to send an email. If you call, we'll just ask you to send the email so we can discuss it." He recently used a pitch from a PR person who made advance arrangements for her economist client to be available for comment right after a Federal Reserve interest rate announcement.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Overseeing all gardening coverage. "We focus on how to do it yourself, not how to buy it. We want to demystify the process [of gardening], explain how to fit the hobby into your lifestyle and make it not complicated," he says. "We still like to show inspirational gardens and landscapes, but the trend is now toward those that are more doable and attainable--not just pretty pictures, but something our readers can actually accomplish," Beckman says. "In some ways, that [trend] crosses over into our other coverage areas, such as food, decorating and crafts. It's no longer about big overwrought things that are just pretty--tell me instead about unintimidating things our readers can do themselves." He recommends attending industry trade events, where the mag usually sends "at least one editor." Lead time is up to a year in advance.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Fishko hosts "The Fishko Files," which covers art, music, media and culture. She does thoughtful, essay-style pieces on a cultural phenomenon or idea, sound-rich profiles of classical or jazz musicians, looks at 3 or 4 films and how they relate to each other and author interviews. "I tend to specialize in cultural history," she says. "I often do pieces about early recordings, old films, artistic pioneers and making-of features about histories works of theatre or film. It's wrong to assume that everything on the radio is a live, daily show. Mine is not live, it is not daily and it is not a show. Rather, it's a series of produced cultural features that appear during Public Radio news programs." A PR pitch about a string quartet that had commissioned an instrument-maker to create all four of their instruments from the wood of the same tree resulted in a story. Contact by email.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Larson covers consumer technology and environmental/green stories. That can include new electronics, HDTV, recycling programs and hybrid cars. "I like pitches that are simple and quick," he says. "I need hard facts, not a long story with a lot of company information. I cover subjects from a consumer standpoint." Stories need to be visual for TV. "Too often stories are pitched that just don't work in the medium of television, not for lack of interest, but for lack of something to see to help tell the story," he says. It's important to have a New York connection, but his pieces are picked up by Fox News Edge and fed to the station's affiliates. "So, it's also a win if it's happening here and happening elsewhere or has a national impact," he says. Avoid repeated pitches or following-up on pitches. A pitch about a student robotics competition turned into a story.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Is also host of "Justice Talking." Seeks "reports that focus on multiple interviews that connect politics, culture, science, ideas." Also, "anything to do with science fiction, research into DNA, the brain, robots, artificial intelligence and artificial life," she says. Whether Adler can sell it to NPR is another matter, but she will definitely listen to your pitch. Know that "anything with any commercial overtones will be rejected immediately, even if it is to support a wonderful cause. This is true about 99.99% of the time." In addition, "anything to do with cosmetics, diet, nutrition, etc. is also probably a no." Contact by "mail or phone. If we are interested in a story we will then also use email and fax."
